subtitle: Practice Set: Problems and Complete Solutions
intro: Work the following ten problems in order. They climb from reading the harmonics of a signal and computing Euler--Fourier coefficients, through even/odd symmetry and half-range expansions, into separation of variables and the Laplacian, and up to a complete separation-of-variables solution of the heat equation with every coefficient integral expanded, plus the d'Alembert solution of the wave equation.

takeaway.title: Unit Key Takeaway
takeaway.body: One idea organizes the whole unit: \highlight{decompose into frequencies, then solve frequency by frequency}. Fourier series rebuild a function from orthogonal sines and cosines, with symmetry choosing the cosine or sine form and half-range extensions adapting it to a boundary. For PDEs, \highlight{separation of variables} turns the equation into the Unit~17 \highlight{eigenvalue BVP} plus a time ODE, superposition assembles the modes, and the \highlight{initial condition} is fitted by a Fourier series. The \highlight{Laplacian} is the shared spatial operator behind the heat, wave, and Laplace equations --- diffusion that smooths, vibration that propagates, and the harmonic steady state.

problems.problem: \strong{(Harmonics)} For $f(x)=4-3\cos(2\pi x)+\sin(\pi x)$ with period $2$ ($L=1$), state $a_0$, the nonzero $a_n$, and the nonzero $b_n$.
problems.solution:
Match $f=\frac{a_0}{2}+\sum_n(a_n\cos n\pi x+b_n\sin n\pi x)$ with $L=1$. The constant $\tfrac{a_0}{2}=4$, so
\[ \work{a_0=8; \qquad a_2=-3 \ (\text{from } -3\cos 2\pi x); \qquad b_1=1 \ (\text{from } \sin\pi x).} \]
All other coefficients are $0$.

problems.problem: \strong{(Euler--Fourier setup)} Write the Euler--Fourier formulas for $a_n$ and $b_n$ of a $2L$-periodic function, and state the orthogonality relations that justify them.
problems.solution:
For a $2L$-periodic $f$,
\[ \work{a_n=\frac1L\int_{-L}^{L}f\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx,\qquad b_n=\frac1L\int_{-L}^{L}f\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx.} \]
They follow from orthogonality: $\int_{-L}^{L}\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\cos\frac{m\pi x}{L}dx=L\delta_{nm}$, the same for sines, and $\int_{-L}^{L}\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\sin\frac{m\pi x}{L}dx=0$ --- integrating $f$ against one basis wave isolates its coefficient.

problems.problem: \strong{(Odd function series)} Find the Fourier series of the $2\pi$-periodic extension of $f(x)=x$ on $-\pi\le x\le\pi$, computing $b_n$ in full.
problems.solution:
$f(x)=x$ is odd, so $a_n=0$ and only $b_n$ survive. With $L=\pi$, integration by parts gives
\[ \work{b_n=\frac1\pi\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}x\sin(nx)\,dx=\frac2\pi\Big[-\frac{\pi}{n}(-1)^n\Big]=(-1)^{n+1}\frac2n.} \]
Hence $x=\sum_{n\ge1}(-1)^{n+1}\frac2n\sin(nx)=2(\sin x-\tfrac12\sin2x+\tfrac13\sin3x-\cdots)$.

problems.problem: \strong{(Symmetry shortcut)} Without integrating, explain why $f(x)=x^2$ on $-\pi\le x\le\pi$ has $b_n=0$ for all $n$, and which series type it has.
problems.solution:
$f(x)=x^2$ is \emph{even} ($f(-x)=f(x)$). The integrand $x^2\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}$ is then odd, so
\[ \work{b_n=\frac1L\int_{-L}^{L}x^2\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=0 \quad(\text{odd integrand over a symmetric interval}).} \]
It therefore has a pure \emph{cosine} series (only $a_0$ and $a_n$).

problems.problem: \strong{(Half-range sine)} Expand $f(x)=1$ on $0\le x\le L$ in a half-range sine series, giving $b_n$ and the resulting series.
problems.solution:
Half-range sine: $b_n=\frac2L\int_0^L\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx$. Then
\[ \work{b_n=\frac2L\cdot\frac{L}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big)=\frac{2}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big)=\frac{4}{n\pi}\ (n\text{ odd}),\ 0\ (n\text{ even}).} \]
So $1=\frac4\pi\sum_{k\ge0}\frac{1}{2k+1}\sin\frac{(2k+1)\pi x}{L}$ on $0<x<L$.

problems.problem: \strong{(Separation of variables)} Separate $u_t=\alpha^2 u_{xx}$ via $u=X(x)T(t)$; state the two ODEs and, with $u(0,t)=u(L,t)=0$, the spatial eigenpairs and time factor.
problems.solution:
Substituting $u=XT$ into $u_t=\alpha^2u_{xx}$ and dividing by $\alpha^2XT$ gives $\frac{T'}{\alpha^2T}=\frac{X''}{X}=-\lambda$. Thus
\[ \work{\begin{gathered} X''+\lambda X=0,\ X(0)=X(L)=0\ \Rightarrow\ \lambda_n=\Big(\tfrac{n\pi}{L}\Big)^2,\ X_n=\sin\tfrac{n\pi x}{L}; \\ T'+\alpha^2\lambda_n T=0\ \Rightarrow\ T_n=e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2t}. \end{gathered}} \]

problems.problem: \strong{(Laplacian / harmonic)} Determine whether $u(x,y)=x^2-y^2$ and $v(x,y)=e^{x}\sin y$ are harmonic by computing $\nabla^2$ of each.
problems.solution:
For $u=x^2-y^2$: $u_{xx}=2$, $u_{yy}=-2$, so
\[ \work{\nabla^2u=2-2=0 \ (\text{harmonic}).} \]
For $v=e^x\sin y$: $v_{xx}=e^x\sin y$, $v_{yy}=-e^x\sin y$, so
\[ \work{\nabla^2v=e^x\sin y-e^x\sin y=0 \ (\text{also harmonic}).} \]

problems.problem: \strong{(Classification)} Classify $u_t=\alpha^2u_{xx}$, $u_{tt}=c^2u_{xx}$, and $u_{xx}+u_{yy}=0$ as parabolic, hyperbolic, or elliptic, and name the phenomenon each models.
\strong{(Heat equation capstone)} Solve $u_t=\alpha^2u_{xx}$ on $0\le x\le L$, $u(0,t)=u(L,t)=0$, $u(x,0)=100$, computing the coefficients in full.
problems.solution:
Separation gives $X_n=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}$, $T_n=e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2t}$, so $u=\sum_n b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2t}$. Matching $u(x,0)=100$,
\[ \work{b_n=\frac2L\int_0^L 100\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=\frac{200}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big)=\frac{400}{n\pi}\ (n\text{ odd}),\ 0\ (n\text{ even}).} \]
\[ \work{u(x,t)=\frac{400}{\pi}\sum_{k\ge0}\frac{1}{2k+1}\sin\frac{(2k+1)\pi x}{L}\,e^{-\alpha^2((2k+1)\pi/L)^2t}\ \xrightarrow{t\to\infty}0.} \]

problems.problem: \strong{(Wave equation)} Use d'Alembert's formula to solve $u_{tt}=c^2u_{xx}$ on the line with $u(x,0)=f(x)$ and $u_t(x,0)=0$, and describe the motion.
problems.solution:
With initial velocity $g\equiv0$, d'Alembert's formula reduces to
\[ \work{u(x,t)=\tfrac12\big[f(x-ct)+f(x+ct)\big].} \]
The initial shape splits into two half-amplitude copies, one traveling right at speed $c$ and one left at speed $c$; their sum reconstructs $f$ at $t=0$ and separates thereafter.
